journal-title-group.journal-title: The Journal of Allergy and Clinical Immunology: Global

title-group.article-title: Bronchial thermoplasty for severe asthma: Systematic review and meta-analysis of benefits and harms

author-notes.corresp: ∗Corresponding author: Derek K. Chu, MD, PhD, Department of Medicine and Department of Health Research Methods, Evidence, and Impact, McMaster University, Health Sciences Centre 3V49, 1280 Main St W, Hamilton, ON, Canada, L8S 4K1. chudk@mcmaster.ca

license.license-p: This is an open access article under the CC BY-NC-ND license (http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc-nd/4.0/).

sec.title: Background
sec.p: The efficacy and safety of bronchial thermoplasty (BT) for severe asthma remains unclear.

sec.title: Objective
sec.p: We systematically synthesized the efficacy and safety of BT in patients with severe asthma.

sec.title: Methods
sec.p: As part of the upcoming American Academy of Allergy, Asthma & Immunology/American College of Allergy, Asthma & Immunology Joint Task Force on Practice Parameters severe asthma guidelines, we searched the Medline, Embase, and Central databases to July 29, 2025, for randomized trials comparing BT to control for asthma. Paired reviewers independently screened records and extracted data. Random-effects meta-analyses addressed asthma control (Asthma Control Questionnaire 6 [scale, 0-6; lower better]), asthma-related quality of life (Asthma Quality of Life Questionnaire [range, 1-7; higher better]), severe asthma exacerbations, and harms, stratified by procedural, postprocedural, and overall. The GRADE approach informed certainty-of-evidence ratings (PROSPERO: CRD42023408565).

sec.title: Results
sec.p: Six trials randomized 573 adults. Procedurally, BT likely increases severe exacerbations (incidence rate ratio [IRR] 2.95; 95% CI, 1.20-7.25; risk difference [RD] 0.08 more events per patient-year; moderate certainty) and serious respiratory adverse events (IRR 5.98; 95% CI, 1.07-33.29; RD 0.18 more events per patient-year; moderate certainty). Postprocedurally, BT may reduce severe exacerbations (IRR 0.67; 95% CI, 0.50-0.89; RD 0.67 fewer events per patient-year; low certainty). Overall, BT may improve asthma control (mean difference −0.37; 95% CI, −0.67 to −0.07; RD for 0.5-point decrease: 15.9% more; low certainty), asthma-related quality of life (mean difference 0.55; 95% CI, 0.28-0.82; RD for 0.5-point increase: 19.1% more; low certainty), and severe exacerbations (IRR 0.81; 95% CI, 0.68-0.97; RD 0.11 fewer events per patient-year; low certainty).

sec.title: Conclusion
sec.p: Among adults with severe asthma, BT may improve asthma control and asthma-related quality of life and may reduce overall severe exacerbations, but it likely increases procedural severe exacerbations and serious respiratory harms.

def.p: Grading of Recommendations Assessment, Development and Evaluation
Instrument for Assessing the Credibility of Effect Modification Analyses

body.p: Asthma is a chronic airway disorder marked by variable and recurring airflow obstruction, heightened bronchial responsiveness, and inflammation. Globally, it affects approximately 262 million individuals. In the United States alone, the estimated 20-year economic burden of asthma approaches $1 trillion,1 encompassing both direct medical costs and productivity losses due to missed work and school days.2 Individuals with severe asthma, defined as disease requiring high-dose inhaled corticosteroids plus a second controller medication and/or systemic corticosteroids, comprise 5% to 10% of the overall asthma population.3 This subgroup bears a disproportionately high burden, incurring nearly triple the direct health care costs, over 9 times more missed work and school days, and 10 times higher indirect costs per person per year compared to individuals with mild asthma.2

Bronchial thermoplasty (BT) is a minimally invasive bronchoscopic procedure performed under moderate or deep sedation.4 It applies thermal energy to the airways to reduce bronchial hyperreactivity and inflammation, reduce the mass of airway smooth muscle and its release of mast cell–activating and proinflammatory mediators, and inhibit mediators involved in fibroblast proliferation in airway epithelial cells.5 These combined mechanisms aim to remodel the airway structure, thereby decreasing the frequency and severity of asthma symptoms and exacerbations.

The benefits and risk of BT in treating severe asthma, however, remain uncertain. Earlier reviews focused exclusively on studies used for initial marketing approval and did not account for recent randomized trials evaluating their efficacy and safety.6 This omission limits the confidence in effect estimates and evidence generalizability. Moreover, none of the reviews used optimal statistical methods, such as adjusting for baseline asthma severity to synthesize outcomes like asthma control and asthma-related quality of life.

As part of updating the American Academy of Allergy, Asthma & Immunology and American College of Allergy, Asthma & Immunology Joint Task Force on Practice Parameters severe and difficult-to-treat asthma guidelines, we performed a systematic review of the efficacy and safety of BT for severe asthma.

sec.title: Methods
sec.p: This systematic review and meta-analysis followed Cochrane Collaboration,7 GRADE (Grades of Recommendation Assessment, Development, and Evaluation),8 and PRISMA (Preferred Reporting Items for Systematic Reviews and Meta-analysis)9 guidance (see Appendix E1 in this article’s Online Repository available at www.jaci-global.org). This review follows similar methods to previous task force reviews10, 11, 12, 13, 14, 15, 16, 17 and was prospectively registered in PROSPERO (International Prospective Register of Systematic Reviews; CRD42023408565).

sec.title: Search strategy and inclusion criteria
sec.p: We searched the Medline, Embase, and Central databases from January 1, 2014, to July 29, 2025, for published and unpublished randomized controlled trials (RCTs) comparing the use of BT to sham therapy or standard of care (see Appendix E2 in the Online Repository available at www.jaci-global.org). We included trials published before 2014 from a previous systematic review.6 To identify additional potentially relevant studies, we cross-referenced other systematic reviews,6,18,19 conducted forward and backward citation analyses of all included studies using the Web of Science (all databases), and consulted with clinical experts from the linked guideline panel.

sec.title: Data collection
sec.p: Paired and calibrated reviewers (D.G.R., A.W.L.C., L.O., M.M.W., X.C.) independently screened titles and abstracts and reviewed full text with Covidence software (Veritas Health Innovation, Melbourne, Victoria, Australia). These reviewers independently extracted bibliographic information, study design, participant characteristics, outcomes according to the intention-to-treat principle, and sources of funding. They resolved disagreements by consensus, and, if necessary, through discussion with a third reviewer (D.K.C.).
For multiple records pertaining to the same trial (eg, conference abstracts, post hoc analyses of included trials), after collecting all relevant data, we conducted analysis as a single study.

When studies presented outcome data only in figures, we extracted data with WebPlotDigitizer v5.2 software (Automeris, Pacifica, Calif). For trials that did not report means or standard deviations (SDs), we imputed data according to Furukawa et al,20 Weir et al,21 and Cochrane guidance.7 If a trial reported data as median and interquartile range or range, we assessed skewness, and if not present, we converted values to means and SDs according to Shi et al22 and Cochrane guidance.7 If present, we estimated mean and SD values by using quantile estimation, Box-Cox transformation, or approximate Bayesian computation, preferring the estimate that was the most consistent with other values within and between trials. We contacted authors for clarification in case of missing, unpublished, or unclear data (see Appendix E3 in the Online Repository available at www.jaci-global.org).

sec.title: Outcomes
sec.p: In accordance with fulfilling trustworthy guideline development processes, we discussed with our multistakeholder guideline development group, including asthma experts (respirologists and allergists), frontline clinicians (pediatricians, internists, physician assistants, nurses and nurse practitioners, and asthma educators), and patient and caregiver partners, to prioritize patient-important outcomes.23,24 We assessed asthma control, asthma-related quality of life, severe asthma exacerbations, respiratory-specific serious adverse events (excluding severe asthma exacerbations), respiratory-specific adverse events (moderate [discomfort enough to cause interference with patients’ usual activities] and severe [incapacitating, with inability to do work or usual activities]), all-cause serious adverse events, and all-cause mortality. Appendix E4 in the Online Repository available at www.jaci-global.org describes relevant outcome measures. Where possible, we stratified analyses for outcomes occurring procedurally (during 6 weeks of treatment and up to 6 weeks after treatment), postprocedurally (after 6 weeks’ posttreatment), and overall. We analyzed outcomes at the longest duration of randomized follow-up.

sec.title: Risk of bias assessment
sec.p: Paired and calibrated reviewers (L.O., M.M.W.) assessed risk of bias independently for each trial outcome by a modified Cochrane Risk of Bias v2 tool for randomized trials.25 We subclassified “some concerns” judgments as “some concerns, probably high” and “some concerns, probably low.” If at least one domain was high or probably high risk, we considered the outcome at high risk of bias for that trial. We assessed publication bias by evaluating the relationship between the results and study funding.

sec.title: Data analysis
sec.p: We summarized continuous outcomes as mean differences (MD) with 95% confidence intervals (CIs) using generic inverse variance random-effects models with restricted maximum likelihood estimators of variance to meta-analyze ANCOVA change from baseline values. If the instruments used to measure an outcome varied between trials, we followed GRADE guidance to convert each measure to the one most familiar to clinicians according to the established minimally important difference (MID) of each measure.26 To facilitate interpretation, we performed responder analyses by calculating the risk difference (RD) and 95% CI to obtain an MID. The established MID for the Asthma Control Questionnaire (ACQ-6) and the Asthma Quality of Life Questionnaire (AQLQ) are both 0.5 points.

Using random-effects models with restricted maximum likelihood estimators of variance, we summarized severe asthma exacerbations and safety outcomes as incidence rate ratios (IRRs) and 95% CIs and/or as RDs and 95% CIs (when rare event rates were observed). One-stage (multilevel random intercept random slope) and 2-stage (random effects inverse variance) Poisson models addressed count outcomes (recurrent events as in asthma exacerbations and safety outcomes) for comparative and single-arm meta-analyses.

We evaluated the certainty (quality) of the evidence with the GRADE approach,8,27, 28, 29, 30, 31, 32, 33 including assessment of risk of bias, imprecision, inconsistency, indirectness, and publication bias; we report our findings using standardized terminology.34 We interpret and present findings with a target of certainty rating of a nonzero effect35 (ie, any benefit or any harm, relative to sham therapy or standard care) and calculated the number needed to treat or number needed to harm for each outcome.36

We performed prespecified subgroup analyses on age (<65 years vs ≥65 years), risk of bias (low vs high), and baseline eosinophil level (<150 cells/μL vs ≥150 cells/μL and <300 cells/μL vs ≥300 cells/μL). We hypothesized that relative effects would be greater among older adults, among trials at high risk of bias, and among participants with higher eosinophil counts. We appraised the credibility of subgroup effects by the Instrument for Assessing the Credibility of Effect Modification Analyses (ICEMAN).37 We performed additional sensitivity analyses to test the robustness of results by multiple data transformation and imputation methods.
We conducted analyses by Stata v18 software (StataCorp, College Station, Tex).

sec.title: Results
sec.p:
Our systematic search identified 1459 unique records, 39 potentially relevant full-text records, and ultimately included 6 relevant RCTs (Fig 1).38, 39, 40, 41, 42, 43 The trials randomized 573 adults, of which 334 were assigned to BT. Table I summarizes the characteristics of the trials and their participants. The trials included participants with a median of reported mean age of 43.1 years (range, 40.5 to 49.7), and a median 58% of participants were female (range, 48% to 80%). The median trial duration after BT administration was 52 weeks (range, 4 to 52).Fig 1
PRISMA (Preferred Reporting Items for Systematic Reviews and Meta-analysis) flow diagram of systematic review of BT for severe asthma.Table I
Characteristics of included trials investigating BT for severe asthma
StudyTrial nameRegistrationNo. randomizedAge (years), mean (SD)Female sex, n/N (%)Duration of follow-up (weeks)Control intervention
Castro (2010)38AIR2NCT0023111429740.7 (11.9)169/288 (59%)52Sham
Cox (2007)39AIRNCT0021452611240.5 (11.3)62/109 (57%)52Standard care
Goorsenberg (2021)40TASMANCT022253924045.5 (11.9)29/40 (73%)26Standard care
Leroux (2024)41THERMASCORTNCT024649953049.7 (8.9)24/30 (80%)52Standard care
Lv (2021)42—Not reported6048.5 (5.4)29/60 (48%)4Standard care
Pavord (2007)43RISANCT002145393440.7 (12.6)16/32 (50%)52Standard care

Objective outcomes, including serious adverse events and mortality, were at overall low risk of bias. Asthma control, asthma-related quality of life, and severe asthma exacerbations were at overall high risk of bias due to a lack of blinding through sham control for most trial participants (see Tables E1 and E2 in the Online Repository available at www.jaci-global.org). We found no strong evidence of publication bias.

sec.title: Outcomes
sec.p:
Table II presents the summary of findings for the efficacy and safety of BT for the management of severe asthma.Table II
Summary of findings table of efficacy and safety of BT for severe asthma
Outcome (time frame)∗Study parameters and measurement scale (no. of participants)Relative effect (95% CI)Absolute effect estimatesNNT/NNHCertainty of evidence (reason)Plain-language summary
ControlBT
Severe asthma exacerbationsOverall (4 to 58 weeks)6 RCTs (536)IRR 0.81 (0.68 to 0.97)0.58 events per patient-year0.47 events per patient-year15Low (serious risk of bias and serious indirectness)†BT may reduce overall severe asthma exacerbations
0.11 fewer (0.19 to 0.02 fewer)
Procedural (up to 6 weeks after last BT)4 RCTs (467)IRR 2.95 (1.20 to 7.25)0.05 events per patient-year0.13 events per patient-year12Moderate (serious risk of bias)‡BT likely increases severe asthma exacerbations during procedural period
0.08 more (0.01 to 0.28 more)
Asthma control (26 to 58 weeks)5 RCTs (476), ACQ-6 (0 to 6 [lower better]), MID = 0.5RR 1.42 (1.08 to 1.75)MD −0.37 (−0.67 to −0.07)6Low (serious risk of bias and serious imprecision)§BT may improve asthma control
Proportion improving by MID
38.0%53.9%
15.9% more (3.0% to 28.4% more)
Asthma-related quality of life (4 to 58 weeks)6 RCTs (536), AQLQ (1 to 7 [higher better]), MID = 0.5RR 1.43 (1.22 to 1.62)MD 0.55 (0.28 to 0.82)5Low (serious risk of bias and serious imprecision)§BT may improve asthma-related quality of life
Proportion improving by MID
44.7%63.8%
19.1% more (9.9% to 27.6% more)
Procedural severe¶ respiratory adverse events (up to 6 weeks after last BT)3 RCTs (421)IRR 5.19 (2.20 to 12.33)0.13 events per patient-year0.67 events per patient-year9Moderate (serious imprecision)‖BT likely increases severe respiratory adverse events during procedural period
0.54 more (0.29 to 0.93 more)
Procedural serious¶ respiratory adverse events (up to 6 weeks after last BT)3 RCTs (421)IRR 5.98 (1.07 to 33.29)0 events per patient-year0.18 events per patient-year25Moderate (serious imprecision)‖BT likely increases serious respiratory adverse events during procedural period
0.18 more (0.06 to 0.30 more)
Relative effect estimates, absolute effect estimates, and NNT/NNH are for important effects, defined as greater than MID for each outcome. NNH, Number needed to harm; NNT, number needed to treat; RR, risk ratio.∗
Number of weeks after last BT procedure.†
We rated down once for serious risk of bias because of most trials’ having high risk of outcome measurement bias due to lack of blinding of trial participants, and we rated down once because of indirectness due to varying follow-up periods (including procedural and postprocedural periods) across included trials.‡
We rated down once for serious risk of bias because of most trials’ being at high risk of bias for measurement of outcome due to lack of blinding of trial participants.§
We rated down once for serious risk of bias because of most trials’ having high risk of outcome measurement bias due to lack of blinding of trial participants, and one for imprecision due to insufficient optimal information size (n = 1226 for simple t test to detect small between-group MD of 0.125).¶
Similar estimates for procedural moderate severity respiratory adverse events (NNH 4, high certainty). Serious = FDA definition. Severe indicates incapacitating; moderate, interfering with usual activities.‖
We rated down once for serious imprecision due to small number of events occurring across included trials.

sec.title: Severe asthma exacerbations
sec.p:
Six trials (n = 536) informed effects of BT on overall severe asthma exacerbations (Fig 2, A). BT may reduce overall severe asthma exacerbations (IRR 0.81; 95% CI, 0.68 to 0.97; rate difference, 0.11 fewer events per patient-year; 95% CI, 0.19 to 0.02 fewer; low certainty).38, 39, 40, 41, 42, 43 Most of this reduction occurred during the postprocedural period (6 weeks after BT; IRR 0.67; 95% CI, 0.50 to 0.89; rate difference, 0.67 fewer events per patient-year; 95% CI, 1.02 to 0.22 fewer; low certainty; Fig 2, B), with BT likely increasing severe exacerbations during the procedural period (up to 6 weeks after treatment; IRR 2.95; 95% CI, 1.20 to 7.25; rate difference, 0.08 more events per patient-year; 95% CI, 0.01 to 0.28 more; moderate certainty; Fig 2, C).Fig 2
Forest plot of BT vs control on (A) overall severe asthma exacerbations, (B) postprocedural severe asthma exacerbations (beyond 6 weeks’ treatment), and (C) procedural severe asthma exacerbations (up to 6 weeks after treatment).

sec.title: Asthma control
sec.p:
Five trials (n = 476) informed effects on asthma control, presented as measured by the ACQ-6 (0-6, with lower scores indicating better control; Fig 3, A).38, 39, 40, 41,43 BT may improve asthma control (MD −0.37; 95% CI, −0.67 to −0.07; RD to obtain a decrease of 0.5 points, 15.9% more; 95% CI, 3.0% to 28.4% more; low certainty).Fig 3
Forest plot of BT vs control on (A) asthma control and (B) asthma-related quality of life.

sec.title: Asthma-related quality of life
sec.p: Six trials (n = 536) informed effects on asthma-related quality of life, presented as measured by the AQLQ (1-7, with higher scores indicating better quality; Fig 3, B).38, 39, 40, 41, 42, 43 BT may improve asthma-related quality of life (MD 0.55; 95% CI, 0.28 to 0.82; RD to obtain an increase of 0.5 points, 19.1% more; 95% CI, 9.9% to 27.6%; low certainty).

sec.title: Safety
sec.p: Three trials (n = 492) informed effects on serious adverse events.38,39,43 BT likely increases the frequency of serious respiratory adverse events (excluding severe asthma exacerbations) over a median 52-week follow-up period (IRR 2.95; 95% CI, 1.20 to 7.25; rate difference, 0.01 more events per patient-year; 95% CI, 0 to 0.03 more; moderate certainty; see Fig E1 in the Online Repository available at www.jaci-global.org). Most events occurred during the procedural period up to 6 weeks after treatment (IRR 5.98; 95% CI, 1.07 to 33.29; rate difference, 0.18 events per patient-year; 95% CI, 0.06 to 0.30; see Figs E2-E4 in the Online Repository) compared to the postprocedural period beyond 6 weeks (see Figs E5 and E6 in the Online Repository). Serious respiratory adverse events included atelectasis, hemoptysis, infection, and aspiration events requiring escalation of medical management (see Table E3 in the Online Repository). We found similar findings for all-cause serious adverse events (see Figs E7 and E8 in the Online Repository).

Similarly, compared to control, BT increased procedural severe (incapacitating, with inability to do work or usual activities; IRR 5.19; 95% CI, 2.20 to 12.23; see Fig E9 in the Online Repository available at www.jaci-global.org) and moderate (discomfort enough to cause interference with patients’ usual activities; IRR 2.40; 95% CI, 1.69 to 3.41; see Fig E10 in the Online Repository) respiratory adverse events. Most adverse events were reported as occurring within 1 day of the BT procedure and resolving on average within 7 days. Events requiring hospitalization or prolonging it resolved with standard therapy (eg, when indicated, hemoptysis managed with bronchial artery embolization).

sec.title: Mortality
sec.p: Three trials (n = 421) informed effects on overall mortality.38,39,43 One death was reported across the trials, attributed to a motor vehicle accident in a patient receiving BT. BT likely does not increase overall mortality (RD 0.4% more; 95% CI, 1.3% fewer to 2.1% more; moderate certainty; see Fig E11 in the Online Repository available at www.jaci-global.org).

sec.title: Subgroup and sensitivity analyses
sec.p: Overall, subgroup analyses showed no differential treatment effects according to risk of bias or baseline eosinophil level (see Tables E4 and E5 in the Online Repository available at www.jaci-global.org). We were unable to perform subgroup analyses because of insufficient data reported between age groups, despite multiple attempts to contact study authors for age data (Appendix E3). Sensitivity analyses demonstrated robustness across different data analysis and imputation strategies.

sec.title: Discussion
sec.p: This systematic review and meta-analysis of 6 RCTs involving 573 participants found that BT may reduce overall severe asthma exacerbations and may improve asthma control and asthma-related quality of life (low certainty), but it likely increases the risk of procedural severe asthma exacerbations and serious respiratory adverse events (moderate certainty).

Key strengths of this review include its being the most comprehensive analysis of BT in the management of severe asthma, incorporating all available RCTs investigating patient-important outcomes; its analysis of unpublished data from trial investigators to differentiate asthma exacerbations stratified by period (procedural, postprocedural, and overall); its incorporation of multidisciplinary input from asthma experts, frontline clinicians, and patient and caregiver partners;23,24 and its interpretation of the results by using structured GRADE8 and ICEMAN37 approaches and meeting GRADE requirements in full.44

Limitations of this review arise from constraints of the available evidence. First, BT has been shown to reduce both serum45 and bronchoalveolar lavage46 fluid eosinophil counts. Observational data have shown conflicting findings on the influence of eosinophilia toward clinical response to BT, with some studies suggesting a greater benefit in eosinophilic asthma and others suggesting greater benefit in noneosinophilic asthma.47,48 Only one trial, however, stratified analyses by baseline eosinophil levels.40 Communication with the company that did the AIR, AIR2, and RISA trials revealed that its trial statistician retired and its data were archived, noting that it would take several years to identify, clean, and transfer the data, so it was not feasible to share the information for our analyses (as outlined in Appendix E3). Consequently, our findings may not adequately represent the subgroup of patients with severe asthma who could derive the greatest benefit from BT. Given that BT is an invasive and resource-intensive intervention, future trials should report baseline eosinophil levels to better identify which patient subgroups benefit most from the therapy, and until future research provides clarity, candidate selection should be driven by response to known predictors49 as well as patient values and preferences.50 Second, 5 of the 6 trials lacked blinding of participants and outcome assessors, leading to a high risk of bias in measures of asthma control, asthma-related quality of life, and possibly severe exacerbations. The subgroup analyses, however, suggested low credibility for effect modification due to risk of bias. Recent methodologic studies have found no significant differences in treatment effects between blinded and unblinded studies, particularly for more objective outcomes such as severe asthma exacerbations, potentially questioning the necessity of using sham controls for procedural interventions in specific scenarios and outcomes.51, 52, 53 Despite this, given the potential influence of performance and detection bias in an unblinded setting, we perhaps overcautiously rated down the certainty of the evidence to account for the potential serious risk of bias. Third, no trials compared BT to other established therapies for severe asthma, such as biologics alone or in combination with BT. Given the complex nature of severe, treatment-resistant asthma, large, robust trials are needed to address key evidence gaps.3

The findings that BT may improve asthma control and asthma-related quality of life align with findings suggested in prior reviews.6,54 However, the applicability of previous findings was questioned because appraisals occurred before most patients initiated therapy with biologics. Expanding the applicability of BT, our updated review incorporates new trials that include patients receiving concurrent biologics and showed consistent effects with the previous trials. Moreover, we were able to systematically evaluate BT's effect on exacerbations by period (procedural, postprocedural, and overall) and found low-certainty evidence that BT may reduce the risk of long-term severe asthma exacerbations. These findings are consistent with observational studies,55 including the PAS2,56 BTGR,57 UKSAR,58 and BT10+59 cohorts, which reported sustained clinical benefits over up to 10 years after BT and similarly high rates of procedural serious respiratory adverse events (PAS2: 63 events in 279 patients; 0.23 events per patient; UKSAR: 14 events in 128 patients; 0.11 events per patient; BTGR: 44/157 [28.0%] patients experiencing one or more events). Additionally, the PAS256 and BTGR57 cohorts evaluated the long-term safety of BT, with low rates of serious respiratory adverse events at year 3 (17/250 [6.8%] patients experiencing one or more events; 0.10 events per patient) and year 5 (11/235 [4.7%] patients experiencing one or more events; 0.06 events per patient) after treatment.56 However, data from the BT10+ cohort found that 6 (6.7%) of 89 patients receiving BT developed new bronchiectasis during long-term follow-up, with no control participants developing bronchiectasis (0 of 18 patients).59 Thus, compared to anti-inflammatory therapy alone, the lung changes, if any, at 10 years or more after BT remain uncertain.

When considering BT for individual patients, the possible long-term improvements in a reduction in severe exacerbations, asthma symptom control, and asthma-related quality of life must be weighed against its potential procedural and long-term harms, resource implications, accessibility, and alignment with patient values and preferences. BT is a resource-intensive intervention requiring a skilled bronchoscopist, an anesthesiologist capable of administering moderate to deep sedation, coadministration of systemic corticosteroids, and typically 3 bronchoscopic sessions. This may confine BT to specialized centers. It is contraindicated in patients with implantable devices or those whose condition is too unstable to permit sedation and bronchoscopy, and it requires travel to designated facilities—factors that limit accessibility. Further, situations in medicine where there is uncertainty in the trade-off between treatment benefits and harms leads to preference-sensitive decisions and the need for personalization through shared decision-making.24,33 Therefore, patients with severe asthma that is refractory to standard care, patients who can access BT easily, and patients who prioritize low-certainty benefits in exacerbation reduction over the burdens and harms of added systemic corticosteroid bursts, moderate-certainty short-term harms of atelectasis, hemoptysis, infection, and aspiration events and uncertain long-term risks of bronchiectasis may favor BT. Conversely, those who are harm/risk averse, steroid avoidant, or face barriers accessing BT may prefer alternative severe asthma treatments with more favorable safety and availability profiles, including add-on long-acting muscarinic antagonists,15,60 macrolide antibiotics,61 and biologics.62, 63, 64 These findings highlight the need for updated guidelines that address the role of BT in severe asthma management and for clinicians to integrate patient values and preferences into the clinical decision-making process.50

At the systems level, policymakers, health systems, and insurers must weigh broader considerations. Notably, Boston Scientific, the manufacturers of the US Food and Drug Administration–approved Alair thermoplasty device, announced plans to discontinue servicing these systems by the end of 2025 because of evolving regulatory requirements and market conditions. However, a 1:1 randomized trial (BATA, NCT03765307) of 160 patients with severe asthma that is still symptomatic despite treatment with high-dose inhaled corticosteroids and long-acting β-agonists is comparing the similarity of Boston Scientific’s Alair system to SyMap’s Elation Bronchial Ablation system, with the primary end point being the rate of severe exacerbations per year over 12 months. This study’s planned completion date is in December 2028, and the findings of this trial and our review may inform further research and development of the Elation and other thermoplasty systems.Key messages
•
In adults with severe asthma, BT may improve asthma control and quality of life and may reduce severe exacerbations in the long term.•
During the procedural period, BT likely increases severe exacerbations and serious respiratory adverse events.

sec.title: Disclosure statement
sec.p: This work was commissioned by the American Academy of Allergy, Asthma & Immunology and the American College of Allergy, Asthma & Immunology through the Joint Task Force on Practice Parameters to inform upcoming guidance on management of severe asthma. The funder contributed to defining the scope of the review but otherwise had no role in study design, data collection, or data interpretation.

Disclosure of potential conflict of interest: P. Bonta reports an investigator-initiated research grant from Boston Scientific for the TASMA trial. P. L. Shah reports investigator roles in the RISA, AIR2 and TASMA trials. L. B. Bacharier reports grants from the National Institutes of Health, National Institute of Allergy and Infectious Diseases, National Heart, Lung, and Blood Institute; personal fees from AstraZeneca, Avillion, Boehringer Ingelheim, Circassia, Genentech/Novartis, GSK, Kinaset, Merck, OM Pharma, Sanofi/Regeneron, Teva, Recludix, and WebMD/Medscape; participation on data safety monitoring boards with AstraZeneca, DBV Technologies, and Vertex; and royalties from Elsevier, outside the current report. The rest of the authors declare that they have no relevant conflicts of interest.

element-citation.article-title: Medical costs and productivity loss due to mild, moderate, and severe asthma in the United States

ack.p: We thank all patients and caregiver partners who helped inform this study and its associated guidelines. We are indebted to the McMaster Health Science librarians for their assistance in retrieving articles and facilitating interlibrary loans. D.C. holds an E. J. Moran 10.13039/100016655Campbell Career Award, Canadian Institutes of Health Research (10.13039/501100000024CIHR) Inclusive Excellence Prize, and CIHR Implementation Science Chair addressing allergy.

fn.p: The first 2 authors contributed equally to this article, and both should be considered first author.
Presented in part at the American Academy of Allergy, Asthma & Immunology 2024 annual scientific meeting; February 23-26, 2024; Washington, DC.
